justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Scheduling lag metric gap identified as the primary SLO gap — called out as CRITICAL with specific recommendation for renewal_scheduling_lag_seconds histogram and p99 > 30min alert. (2) Retry tracking gap explicitly identified with retry_attempts_total, retry_exhausted_total, and retry_queue_depth recommendations. (3) Current alert critiqued as symptom-based — noted it 'is either too noisy (normal card declines trigger it) or too quiet (doesn't fire for the actual failure modes)' and that it cannot answer 'are we meeting our SLO right now?' (4) Specific metric recommendations provided in a structured table with metric names and alert thresholds. (5) SLO-based alerting recommended — burn-rate alerting (Google SRE style, multi-window, multi-burn-rate) listed in nice-to-haves. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough — identified the core SLO measurement gap immediately and made it the central theme ('You have an SLO you cannot measure')
Retry tracking gap clearly identified with specific metrics (retry_attempts_total, retry_exhausted_total, retry_queue_depth) and the revenue impact framed well
Current alert critiqued from multiple angles: may fire on non-issues (card declines) or miss actual failure modes — classic symptom-vs-SLO distinction
Went beyond rubric expectations with additional valuable findings: dead man's switch for scheduler, queue depth metric, health check expansion, circuit breaker absence
Structured recommendations with clear must-have vs nice-to-have prioritization and specific metric names and alert thresholds
SLO burn-rate alerting placed in nice-to-haves rather than must-haves — for a 99.9% SLO this arguably belongs in the critical path, though the scheduling lag alert partially covers this
